title: Enemy Types — Inheritance
description: Create an inheritance hierarchy for game characters. Make GameCharacter a base class with a virtual attack() method. Derive a Player class that overrides attack() (e.g., critical hit chance) and an Enemy class that overrides attack() (e.g., different damage formula). Create at least one more derived enemy type (e.g., Dragon, Boss) with its own attack behavior. Use the protected access specifier for members that derived classes need. Demonstrate polymorphism by calling attack() through a base class pointer or reference. Assign your C++ code to the variable `cpp`.
test_code:
assert 'virtual' in cpp, 'Use virtual keyword for methods that should be overridden'
assert 'override' in cpp or 'protected' in cpp, 'Use override keyword on derived methods or protected for inherited access'
import re
inheritance_matches = re.findall(r'class\s+\w+\s*:\s*public\s+\w+', cpp)
assert len(inheritance_matches) >= 2, f'Create at least 2 derived classes using inheritance (found {len(inheritance_matches)})'
assert 'class Player' in cpp or 'class player' in cpp, 'Create a Player class derived from GameCharacter'
assert 'class Enemy' in cpp or 'class Dragon' in cpp or 'class Boss' in cpp, 'Create at least one enemy class derived from GameCharacter'
assert 'attack()' in cpp, 'Override the attack() method in derived classes'
assert 'GameCharacter' in cpp, 'Use GameCharacter as the base class'
acceptance_criteria: GameCharacter base class has virtual attack() method
Player class derives from GameCharacter and overrides attack()
Enemy class derives from GameCharacter and overrides attack()
At least one additional derived enemy type (Dragon, Boss, etc.)
protected access specifier used for members needed by derived classes
Polymorphism demonstrated via base class reference or pointer in combat()
hints: class Player : public GameCharacter { public: Player(string n, int h, int ap) : GameCharacter(n, h, ap) {} int attack() override { ... } };
Player's attack() could have a 20% critical hit: if (rand() % 5 == 0) return attackPower * 2; else return attackPower;
Dragon's attack() could deal double damage: return attackPower * 2;
The combat() function takes GameCharacter& references — polymorphism means the right attack() is called
Don't forget virtual ~GameCharacter() {} for a virtual destructor
